speaker: Alben Halen
words: But the father that got the cows, he was, he beat me down there when they went, I led the horses down and he come with the truck and gonna get his cattle. We had to push one or two of them young ones into the barn. You know, I done pretty good at that. I sold a saddle horse that, five year old, I got $50 for it. And I sold that to a guy that didn't have a god dang ding to live on. I guess he had about five dollars but I didn't tell him that that's all he paid down on the horse. But I knew he was good but he was working on that job and I was going to put him off and that come to me so excited that he bought a horse from you. Yes, he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did you pay for it?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I said he didn't pay a god dang cent. So I got to go back on the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to work on that on that government work?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: But I had plenty of stuff to eat and I had to eat stuff for the horses and cows. I always was shaking, I always had kept plenty of grain. No, and I took him put the horns on the three -year -old, drove him down a time or two in the field with an old horse. I had a come up and I stayed about a dollar for him. He took out the money and paid me. Then at a two -year -old I kept that with spring and I had, so I could shift an old horse off and I thought I'd work early. Well then my brother -in -law he'd come up. You take my horses and then you got three years. You can run three foot burners and you put in my carpe at about 25 acres to put in on the 40. Well I got them horses and then that young one had to go right in with the rest of them working. You know, that was the best little animal I ever drove.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And I rented a lot of ground there too, you know, at that horse place. And two other places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Harshes' Place?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, he had a farm next to me on the north. He overtook for jets I guess. I left and my brother, I was cut in piece, 110 acres piece and I went up to four o 'clock in the morning and started cutting and I had a kid bringing my breakfast and then I had a team at eight o 'clock in the morning and I ate my breakfast and he unhooked the horses and put the others off while I was eating and I went till noon while he came out the horses were had in the morning and I ate my dinner and he come back up with the other team at four o 'clock a little before and then I worked till seven or I quit. But I cut 20 acres with five and a half foot more every darn day. Well that sounds like a lot. With horses it is. And that's a job to hold them on that carrier and bunch them when they're gone. And then after that while he was cutting grain I had to go and shock back so he could get through. I slept a couple hours but 120 acres when I cut 28. Wasn't taking long. And he was meowing about them horses. There was no good. Finally I met the best horses. But I said I got to have them at least two days more. Well that's too long. I said take the two -year -old and put it on the grain and put the other one of mine on the on the tongue on the binding. Put the coat next to the grain. Oh he says you'll help me hook her up. I said hook her up heck. I said nothing. But I said you start up my mind and make some noise. Why she get a little nervous why stop. She even got she knew there was something wrong behind she laid her feet down like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gonna make a lunge and I said Betty what's the matter with you?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: She was in a pattern they had and went around. We crawled up and got the lines and he drove up 100 feet and stopped. She looked around. You know she had them reels coming back of her. But it didn't water her. After that she come to a corner by them two and take that truck a goddamn short. She just barely missed the reels. Instead of when they give it to pull the platform just flew right back.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: After he had them the day of I says do you want the other horses pretty soon now?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No heck he said I'm gonna keep her on there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made her such a good horse?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I wonder why she was so good a horse.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well I don't know she didn't I put her wit as a saddle horse. I told her it's just a two -year -old and I come and tree in in August I believe and I said to my brother's wife we was working together and I says I couldn't leave that totally and go in there and that last up and went to her work. And he'd go home and feed my chickens and this and that around there at noon and I says bring the harness over. Her harness I had the harness picks up her and oh that's what I gonna do with her I said I'm gonna hook her on the plow. On the foot burners because you know what stumpy ground we plowed.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well she come up and looked at it and I said she better give me a whip and I laughed I knew I wasn't going to have to whip her and he says I start off the head of you and then I put her on the inside on the furrow and when I turned on the corners well you know they're coming right against I thought probably she wouldn't get out away from the tub so heck she applied a half a day with her and put her in and then the next day I come out for you know she got sore in her mouth. I stood and looked at her and I left the altar on and I snapped the reins to the lines to the altar. No it's an animal I broke lots of horses but nothing like her but I worked with that same horse. He was a good horse and broke all kinds of cold wood and she worked beside of it and I got home to plow some and follow her. I was plowing that alone I had more horses than I know what to do with so I turned her in the pasture but when I come going home at noon why where that horse was there where she was he went on that side of him and she walked up with me took him to some water she walked up to the barn and I leave the bars down turned them in and she walked into her stall.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: But you had to be with that horse that she had worked with. She wanted to stay beside of the horse that she had worked with when I took her home. I had done that you know when she was working I she was worked beside the horse and I left them home. She got in there beside the horse and I didn't even lead her but she followed just like I was leading her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So do you always try to work them two together? Did you try to work them two together?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I worked with her beside of the horse all spring work but after that I shifted her back and forth anywhere it didn't make any difference. He decided to put her with. It always looked like that she had been the mother to it that about. They had her jiggerooed out. He worked around the window and went to get all the wood with her by all the done they run her back and forth just laying on that trough. But after I got to working her she turned out she was awful fast, fast walker. And so was this young one she just fall right up on the thing she wouldn't hang on the bit any. They looked alike and I don't know I had to put a year step on it and cut a whole front put off by the shooter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she do that?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: She cut her foot off and fought with other horses and got up, raced up and got the dang thing and sawed it back and forth and pulled the wire fence with her. Cut it off. And I had to order in the night.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Guess I sold her to the fox farm or something. And after that I never had no luck with horses. Of course we had the tractor after that in with them but I bought a big mare for $10 so I weighed 1500 pounds. And I traded horses back and forth. And I missed.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: So I didn't have more and got down to I didn't have more than two of them and one of them got sick and walked around. I walked around. And then after that I... She lay down in the barn and once you go and get up, of course you couldn't take her front feet to head. She kept them and tried to get up. So I struck on them and gave them a jerk ahead.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Why did she fly up like a bullet?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Finally got rid of her too. And I had one that my brother had a horse. A young horse. We got him but we had him many years. You didn't want him over there so I had him to call the liquid and this and that. And he got to Henry when I was going to have him in the middle of the evening while he drove him to the farthest end of the farm.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: So I got rid of them and I never had no horses after that. Oh I traded horses and bought horses and sold horses. My dad left. He left two horses that belonged to him when I rented the place. He says I want $75 a piece for them. No, I didn't give you $75 for them older horses.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He looked at me and he says, I'll give them to you on one agreement. When you're done with them, take them out and shoot them and put them out of misery. Don't let anybody else get them.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And I did. When I was a kid I broke them and I had them for years and years. My oldest brother, he comes about dang mad at you. First he didn't say nothing until I came home with him. I went in the barn and harnessed him up and I took him to the house and showed him to mother what he looked like. And I went and hooked him up and harnessed another one of the other horses and took him out and hooked the lines on him. And I went down and drove him and the lines down to the mailbox. And that's half a mile over to get the mail and got the mail and drove him back. Thought I'm getting used to the bit. And he comes.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He says, do you know that colt would run away with you anytime you wanted to? He says, why don't you time down to the other horse?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Got a good balling off. If I'm ever doing that, I didn't have no trouble with her.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I never had a bad colt, breaking a bad colt. They don't try to run away unless it's some group of some kind. I say, they don't run away the first time you hook them up. They pay attention to the horse that you're working with. I had that old brown horse that he finally got old and he got wobbly. He couldn't hardly get up and fat. But his legs seemed to die out on him when he lay down too long. I finally got rid of him, took him out and shot him and murdered him. Otherwise I sold a lot of horses and traded. I done a good deal on the cows. That way I got a little money, but then I had two left. I went down to my sister and she had married and lived with her.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I asked her, do you want a half of beef?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: She says, I could use it. I said, I'll give you half of beef if you can. You can half of it. Boil it in four hours and seal it and put it in fruit jars. She said, I'll do this. I think I cut meats every three or four days. She canned that and a lot of her own. Made hamburgers and meatballs and put them in fruit jars. So I had plenty to eat anyway.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: When you were working that colt that you got and traded for...

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I bought a dress, but my other two brothers, the one that I bought in with, I paid him so much a year until I paid for the place. I rented it and first I gave him a... He was supposed to have a third. When I rented it, I got a half on his share. On half of the crop, that's what he got. I paid him off and then the rest I paid cash and finished him out. So I had it on my own eyes. Of course he stayed with us until he died. He had about six operations.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Then he broke his hip the last time.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I put in a socket in there, but it took out pieces of guts that long and a piece of that long as a coal one. So does the station between here. Then I sewed them and cut them open and sewed them up because they got a hernia in that cut. Then I put that leg where they cut that, just like mine, and put in a steel joint in there. But that was broken off on his leg, his flop like this, until they done that. Then his guts and then he got that they're... ...shaken. He's that way too, you know. I think I stayed up for nineteen years. With me for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he walk around?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh yeah, he walked around with a cane. But you know, there was everything else the matter with him.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I wonder what made his health go bad.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well I don't know. Nothing go through him, see, when he got that way. He had to douche it up with water and raise cane and fool around with him. Up and around. Oh, as it lived I took him down to the home there. I just went left taking care of him because he'd sleep in the daytime and at night he'd holler and rave about this and that. I never even went to sleep. So you know, that worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which brother was this?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: This is the oldest brother. And then the next brother there. He had a beer pourer up here, one on another. My brother Gust, he was killed in a logging accident. His wife lives out on the farm here at times, but she's working at Pullman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to Gust?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He had a log load.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: It slipped over and when the load split out and he fell on him.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He worked in the woods, well, it was all a day job, but the Oscar didn't, after he got sick, why he didn't work.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He had Social Security and you know that was an awful lot, fifty -seven dollars a month. And after he got it figured out how much he got left, it cost him twenty -five dollars a month for his medicine. Well, you know there wasn't much left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much land were you farming then when you were using horses? How much?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh heck, we had, me and him together, we had a place that owned 160, but 130 acres of field on it. And we farmed another place that Lester Nelson has got. We farmed that, but we had an old fortune tractor at that time too. And we farmed his place, that is 80 acres. Oh, you turn off a lot of work with horses if you've got enough of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many did you usually have then? Horses? Yeah, how many were you using for work?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, I tell you that's all I could, how much I could trade one year when I had that colt while I sold and traded and when I come to spring work. I only had my own place, 130 acres, so I had two colts. I bought, went up and bought a saddle mare up there from Bovill, that butcher up there. And a three year old colt, that's all I had to, and two older horses, older horses. I put in the whole work, of course I had hay too. I was raised, I was at probably 30 acres of hay and 20 acres of summer fallow. I didn't have no trouble putting it in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else? You had 30 acres of hay and 20 acres of summer fallow and what else did you raise?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh, wheat, wheat and oats and barley, wasn't the main crop. I went heavy on four wheels but I got that god dang government stuff, pulled with it. And I had that other place, you know, I had 55 acres of wheat on my own place, average, average year. And you know, when I quit, long before I quit, I kept cutting it down to 18 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean that's all you could plant?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: That's all I could plant and then I had to lay so much ground I had to lower four of that. Over production. I was hoping then that they'd never get a damn thing. There'll never be that again, you know it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well that's crazy, 55 acres of wheat can't be over production.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, you know, I had another place just the same as mine, you know. I'd shift on mine and I'd lay off back and forth. And then I raised some peas and all that went into wheat in the fall.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And I had 120 acres, well you know that's a lot of peas. I didn't have so much on my own place that year anyway, under 20 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you rotating peas and wheat?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, I'd take a crop of peas and then disc up the ground and plant it into wheat. Of course I always had some summer fallow.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: You'd raise good wheat, put fertilizer on every year like they do now and then. Them years they didn't put any fertilizer on. That come on later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you raise a pretty good wheat crop anyway?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well I raised about 35 -40 bushels today and that's good wheat. Lots of them don't raise anymore now with fertilizer on. Oh well, I'll admit that. Straight summer fallow and first fertilizer on. The year that everything went out all over. My wheat went out on, I had 20, I was allowed 22 acres then. I got 65 bushel on 11 acres.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And the other field that I had went 43. And my neighbor, he had a rancher on there and he stayed just late. And you see the spears stand now never two feet. Just winter kill. Well one neighbor there on the west of me, he had, I don't know what the heck, he come up with a long spear so far apart and then you could find a little bunch here and there. They had to disc everything up and put it into spring wheat. Well nowadays you know they're getting, well if it's 3 ,000 acres or more. The fella that sold my place to his dad and the boys then was there 3 -4 ,000 acres. And then at McKinsey he's got no less. Well right around the home there, the place on the north, that's farmed by this big farmer. And then he goes back over and he's got 200 acres that he and others probably used to live on farm. He's got that. And now right on the west, 40 of them, there's another farmer. The same farmer, he's got that farm. 120 acres. But then he jumped over one place. This fella that was renting from me, he was renting that. That's one of the big farmer's boys. He sold that place so he didn't get that. But then he jumped around on the east side of me. He got a farm there, 160 acres. And then on the south, that single, you know, he's a big farmer on Berridge. He owned that place so he didn't get that. And you know, after a little while, he gets a gun like that. They can do as they please if they're processing what they want. They'll just say, we'll cut it out. We'll cut it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: What is the government going to do?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: They can't come and tell you to put that in or we'll shoot you. They're farming it the way they want to. And they'll have to say they're controlled over the whole darn works if they keep it going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid and growing up there, was it all small little farms? Huh? When you were growing up, was it all small farms there?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: It's a farm of 160 acres and less of people living. All of them farms have raised a big family. On the south, there's a family of, south of my place, a family of five, six kids. And cornered into it there was a family of five. And one that comes right down beside of mine there was a family of, on that 60 that goes out, about nine, ten kids. No, they was family on every 160 acres and less.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And now where's that?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: You go down on that thing they call Texas Ridge and look and see how many of that's farming there. There's less than 10, 12 farmers on the whole damn ridge. And there was probably 30, 40 of them.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No things have changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of them people when you were a kid, now what year were you born in again?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, I'm 78 years old. 96.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then, when you were a kid growing up, was it mostly homesteaders that were still there?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, that homesteader, the place that dad got was on that homestead. He come from Sweden, but he went to Minneapolis. Thought he stayed down there and took out his citizen papers and come back up here and got around and filed on that place and stayed here. He didn't have nothing down there, but he took out his citizen papers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he homesteaded here?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, he homesteaded. And so did all the rest of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why he came to Idaho? How he ever came here instead of someplace else?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: That I don't know exactly how that come.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I wonder if you might have known somebody that was here or something.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh, my uncle, he come from Sweden too. He was married to mother's sister. But then the whole darn country along Bear Creek, there was nothing but sweets. Ever darn one day, sat along that creek there so they could have plenty of water. And then this thing they called Pine Creek. That was never dry then. They said there was water, all the timber expanded. Solid timber. There was millions of feet knocked down on the home place. Ringed the trees. Went around and hacked them with knives until they got dry. Took them, put a fire to them, burnt them down and burnt them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now that don't sound right, does it?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Sounds like a big waste.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what the heck they gonna live for?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I hauled wood after I was fifteen years old. And hauled wood up here to Deary. And up along the track by the warehouse they wood piled, left the wood piled. They got seven dollars a cord, six, seven dollars a cord, the whole big price.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Was it lucky if I would have known you were going to buy a lossy cord of wood for about forty dollars, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you clear any of that land or did your father clear it all?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, my father had the most that I cleared. We cleared some patches here and there. He took the best parts, the best ground wash and then he probably left the job with junk. We took all that out after we borscht other farming.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I heard that the land on Bear Ridge, well that's better land than the land on Bear Creek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the Bear Ridge land was settled later, huh?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, this is, they come in, I guess a Bear Ridge down below us, was put in their homestead at the same time. That's figure put a good ground. Well, you know where the division of the divide, lower and upper Bear Ridge, when you go out of town there, where that goes the other way, that's lower Bear Ridge and that's the supper Bear Ridge.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Now it's, I thought I stopped to think, you know, well if them farmers when I started our homestead, they went out and worked outside where they could get work. If I made a hundred dollars, you know, they live on that a year. Where a hundred dollars goes now. My uncle, he took a contract cleaning out that cut by Moscow. He had a farm down there at that time already.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He went bankrupt. Well, my dad worked on the road coming from Moscow to Kendrick. He worked on that road.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He didn't tell me, he told me he worked there, but that drugiest man, not the drugiest that's in Troy, no, he's one before by the name of Johnson too. He says, you're dead, he stayed up my folks and boarded here when he was working on the railroad.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And that's before I was born, I guess, no.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, it was.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, there was only three kids born, Freda and Oscar and Robert, three kids. They come from Sweden and they were, I guess, about four or five years old, oldest one five probably. The rest of the kids was all born out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your uncle, when he went bankrupt, was that on the railroad?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He took the contract to put that up for so much money, to clean that cut out, and he went behind, so he didn't have enough money to pay the workers or not. He had to file bankruptcy. I think that happened to a lot of folks. That took everything away that he had, but he was one of them guys that said, oh, he says, only one thing to do, he says put on new breastlets and start in again.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And he'd buy anything that he could, if he could buy it on time, he'd buy it.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: He had debts that he was told that he quit farming, and then he had a couple of thousand debts to pay on the last place. He bought a place, two, three places, but after he got so far, he sold them and then he bought another one. So he wasn't any farther ahead, but this place, the place he had, no last time, I can't figure out what. I was there when I was a kid, and stayed with Auntie too. And Uncle, but it had two big silos on it, and a big barn. But he lived in town at the time, and went out to the farm and farmed. You know, he was a town man, he'd come home every night and dressed up and went downtown.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: This buy it on time, though, that... He did pretty well. He sold the place, I guess, for, at that time, for 17 or 18 thousand dollars, probably 19, because I think he had 17 thousand dollars left after he paid the debt. Well, you know, that was big money at that time. Sure was. That place out there where you come over to divide, and it goes lengthwise on the side, and it goes right down to the old road. I farmed that, and the fellas, I bought that from the fellas that owned it. Before him, they all thought he got a big price, he sold that for 6 thousand dollars.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Oh, I bought this place to the home place once before. The first World's War, I had saved up a little money and...

speaker: Alben Halen
words: ...tell them the truth about it. And Dad, we got ready to move to town, but he stayed out there anyway.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: What the heck?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Wheat went up, when Dad had the last crop, wheat went up to 2 dollars and 50 cents a bush, after that first World's War. And Dad was one of them fellas that was always lucky anyway, he says. I was hauling in the grain in the fall, he says, whenever you get in there, it's a 250 ton with salt.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And then it took a slump right down like that. Wheat went down to 65 cents a bushel, and oats, I don't know. Less than a cent a bushel, I guess. But God dang it, you ain't got what the heck you're gonna pay with.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: There was a fella down there, I could call his name to it. Well, don't bother his name. He had lots of big crops, he hauled in his crop, hauled it in. It went down to 65 cents. He left the lead there for 4 or 5 years. I guess the story gave it up. I think you get a start of 65 cents, you know, so much a ton a month a year, a month for storage. And you take for 3 or 4 years, oh, I hadn't had any crop yet. Had to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to you when you got the place and the bottom fell out of the market?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, I tried to pay and I thought I'm dead, I bought it from my dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I went to him and I told him, you bought a place?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No, he said I didn't. Yes, you did. I gave him a quick ring deep back on the place. He stood and looked at me a while and said, well,

speaker: Alben Halen
words: he says, that's for the money you paid in, I'll give you a break, that's how you take the crop. So I didn't exactly go broke exactly on it. I got that crop and then I turned. He had it and we farmed it for him for a couple of years, me and my brother Chris.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Then he moved into town and I was ranting at then of course when he moved in.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Made pretty good at that, you know, some years. I raised peace there. Peace on the whole darn thing one time for about four thousand dollars besides the other crop. That's good money at that time. So I shouldn't holler too much.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, when that happened there after World War I, I think it just caught so many people. A lot of people from what I've heard that had gone and got bigger and bought more land and they just went flat broke.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well, it wasn't only me that went broke. I followed down on Berge and went up and bought that place on Texas Ridge, a darn nice place. And sold this place there and paid down so much on it and everything. He made bigger money of course on the last one. He lost everything he could pay.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And he wasn't on doing quite a few others the same way.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: One fella he brought, he had that place next to me there. He over took that.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And this other fella, he had a mortgage on all his cattle, his horses and everything. He got mad and he took and moved them down to his place and says, there you are, do whatever you want to. Put the money he had borrowed on the bank. Well,

speaker: Alben Halen
words: the bank went broke in O 'Hare too.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: I didn't lose such a heck of a lot on it I think I had.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: 15, 16 hundred dollars in here I guess at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you lose that?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well I didn't, I got a little payment. But I had my money on the Troy bank.

speaker: Alben Halen
words: They went in and got the money. Them big money men went in there and the bank was well hooked up. But they had to have so much cash, well they just went in there and put it in the bank. And the bank never went broke in Troy. Didn't have any trouble with that part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the dairy bank a very good bank?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Well they got in bad because they had loaned off money to these renters and stuff. They couldn't pay, he got a farm and this and that, cattle. And he didn't have enough back and forth. They put in about what you call them, to collect the stuff. He collected there for his wages and this and that. Till the people that had money there went in and closed them out. Closed them out and divided up the rest of it. Or he just stayed there yet as long as he could drop one. He says I would have collected it. Well you know I come in too slow to do it. Well, I said people are crazy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That first world war, what was the idea to raise everything up?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: And the wheat, you couldn't save a sack at home, you were supposed to take it to the warehouse. And then when you bought a sack of flour you were supposed to buy that much rice and that much corn and this substitute to go with it. One poor farmer out here, he had bought four sacks of flour and he had laid them at home there. Somebody seen it and went squint on it. He had to take it there and haul it up to town. And now stuff like that, he says people are crazy. That is, that's just crazy. Yeah. And the sugar. The hollering about sugar went up high now. Took and raised that up to twenty, hundred pounds was twenty -five dollars. My dad had them out of cannon, wanted to do them. Me and my brother was working out to come home and my mother was kicking about this and that. Sugar was so high, didn't say nothing. Went up to town and bought a sack of sugar, a hundred pounds of sugar and took it out. He did that, later on in another war, you couldn't buy more so much. You had to get stamps and you, now what the heck the idea of it. A bunch of foolishness, all of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you bought her that sugar you paid twenty -five bucks for a hundred pounds?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: Yeah, we paid twenty -five dollars for a hundred pounds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You bought it anyway?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: We bought it anyway and took it home together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she'd stop complaining?

speaker: Alben Halen
words: No, she was doing a lot of canning and of course, of course we did not work. When we was out and out we would go home to the folks. We had to do something for the board. Of course, like when I was home, generally if I went out in the summer, well then I'd work in the spring and then harvest. Then that is all over and then we're back to the woods.
